title: Smart Cities & Agriculture
concept: Computer vision monitors urban traffic patterns, counts vehicles, assesses crop health, and automates harvesting.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a point cloud in robotic vision?

options.text: A set of 3D coordinates representing physical surfaces
A type of dataset augmentation noise

exercises.explain: Point clouds map 3D structures using coordinates collected from lasers or depth sensors.

cards.title: Farming from the skies
cards.body: How do you check millions of crop plants for disease? You can't walk to every single leaf. Instead, drones equipped with multispectral cameras fly over fields, using computer vision to spot plant stress before it spreads!

cards.title: Vision in Infrastructure & Agriculture
cards.body: In Smart Cities, vision models perform vehicle counting and traffic flow analysis. In Smart Agriculture, models analyze aerial imagery for yield prediction, crop disease, and guide autonomous tractors.

cards.title: A doctor for the fields and roads
cards.body: Think of smart city cameras as traffic doctors measuring road pulse (vehicle count) to adjust stoplight timing. Agricultural vision is like a plant doctor looking at leaf patterns to diagnose nutrient needs.

cards.title: Weed-spraying robots
cards.body: A smart tractor uses high-speed cameras pointing at the ground. A segmentation model identifies weeds in real-time. The system sprays herbicide only on the weed pixels, reducing chemical use by 90%.

cards.title: Weather conditions are a major obstacle
cards.body: You might think outdoor vision is simple. In reality, changes in sun angles, shadows, wind blowing leaves, and rain create immense visual noise that degrades standard indoor-trained model accuracy.

left.text:
Detects cars, bikes, pedestrians
Understands traffic patterns
Adjusts signal timing
Operates 24/7 in rain/snow

right.text:
Detects weeds and crop health
Guides harvesting machinery
Uses multispectral cameras
Handles varying natural light

exercises.prompt: How does computer vision help reduce herbicide usage in smart farming?

options.text: By detecting weeds in real-time and spraying chemical only on them
By genetically modifying the plants to resist bugs
By speeding up the processing of weather reports
By converting models to INT8 precision

exercises.explain: Targeted weed spraying identifies specific weed pixels and activates nozzle sprayers only where needed.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Wind, shadows, and changing sunlight are significant challenges for agricultural vision models deployed outdoors.
exercises.explain: True. Outdoor environments are highly dynamic and introduce visual noise that can confuse models.

exercises.sentence: Analyzing traffic camera feeds to measure flow patterns is part of ___ city design.
exercises.explain: Smart cities integrate active computer vision to monitor and optimize city resources like traffic.

exercises.prompt: Which camera type is best for agricultural drones checking crop health from above?

options.text: Multispectral cameras (capturing visible and near-infrared light)

exercises.explain: Multispectral cameras capture light bands (like near-infrared) that reveal plant chlorophyll and health status.

exercises.prompt: You are designing an agricultural drone to count apple yields on trees. The drone flies under the tree canopy where sunlight creates harsh shadows and highlights. How should you design your training dataset?

options.text: Include photos taken at different times of day, under clouds, and use heavy brightness/contrast data augmentation.
Only use studio photos of perfect apples on white backgrounds.
Train the model on black-and-white cartoon drawings of apples.
Train the model on indoor warehouse images.

exercises.explain: Training with diverse, representative outdoor data and applying photometric augmentations ensures model robustness against natural lighting variations.
